hook: You already know how to read whole numbers like 345, where each digit has a place value — hundreds, tens, and ones. But what happens when a number has a dot in it, like the price $3.75 on a price tag, or a runner's time of $12.08$ seconds? Those digits after the dot represent parts of a whole, and they follow their own place-value system that mirrors the one you already know — but goes in the opposite direction. How do you read and name a decimal like $0.407$ correctly?
concept: A decimal is a way of writing numbers that are between whole numbers, using a dot called the decimal point to separate the whole part from the fractional part. Formally, each digit to the right of the decimal point occupies a place whose value is a power of ten in the denominator: the first place is tenths $\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)$, the second is hundredths $\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)$, the third is thousandths $\left(\frac{1}{1000}\right)$, and so on.
anatomy: A decimal number has two main sections separated by the decimal point. To the left is the whole-number part, read just like any whole number (it can be zero). To the right is the decimal part, made up of individual place-value positions: the tenths place (first digit after the point), the hundredths place (second digit), the thousandths place (third digit), and beyond. When you read a decimal aloud, you say the whole-number part, say 'and' for the decimal point, then read all the digits to the right as a single number followed by the name of the rightmost occupied place — for example, $3.75$ is read 'three and seventy-five hundredths.'

worked_example.description_latex: We start with a simple one-digit decimal. The digit $6$ sits in the tenths place — the first position to the right of the decimal point — so this number is read as 'six tenths,' which equals $\frac{6}{10}$. Identifying which place the last digit occupies tells us the name of the entire decimal.

Here the whole-number part is $4$ and the decimal part has two digits: $3$ in the tenths place and $8$ in the hundredths place. Because the last digit is in the hundredths place, we read the decimal portion as a whole number — 'thirty-eight' — followed by 'hundredths,' giving us 'four and thirty-eight hundredths.' Notice we never say 'four point three eight' in formal mathematical reading.

This three-digit decimal has a zero in the tenths place, a $5$ in the hundredths place, and a $7$ in the thousandths place. Because the last digit reaches the thousandths place, we read all three digits together as the number $507$ and say 'five hundred seven thousandths.' The zero in the middle is essential — dropping it would change $507$ to $57$, giving a completely different value.

The whole part is $12$ and the decimal digits are $0$, $4$, $0$, reaching the thousandths place. Reading the decimal digits as a three-digit number gives $040 = 40$, so this is 'twelve and forty thousandths.' A trailing zero after the last nonzero digit does not change the value, but it does remind us which place we have named — thousandths, not hundredths.

partial_example.description_latex: We are going to read this decimal aloud correctly. First, identify the whole-number part to the left of the decimal point — it is $2$, so we start with 'two and.' Now look at the decimal part: we have three digits, $0$, $8$, $3$, so the last digit is in the thousandths place.

Read all three decimal digits together as a whole number, then attach the place-value name of the rightmost digit. What number do the digits $0$, $8$, $3$ form, and what place-value name should follow it? Write the complete spoken name for $2.083$.

The digits $083$ read as the whole number $83$ (leading zeros are dropped when reading), and since the last digit is in the thousandths place, the full name is 'two and eighty-three thousandths.' Notice that the zero in the tenths place is not ignored when determining the place-value name — it holds the tenths position so that $8$ stays in hundredths and $3$ stays in thousandths.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{The decimal 'nine and fifty-two thousandths' is written as a numeral. What is it? Express it with the correct number of decimal places.}
\text{A swimmer finishes a race in } 58.067 \text{ seconds. Write this time in words as you would read it on a scoreboard.}

common_mistakes: Students read each digit after the decimal point separately — saying 'three point four seven' instead of 'three and forty-seven hundredths.' Fix this by treating all the digits to the right of the decimal as a single whole number and then naming only the rightmost place.

When a zero appears in an interior decimal place (like the tenths place in $0.083$), students skip it and read the number as if it were $0.83$, calling it 'eighty-three hundredths' instead of 'eighty-three thousandths.' Fix this by always counting all digits from the decimal point to determine the correct place-value name.

Students confuse 'tens' with 'tenths' and 'hundreds' with 'hundredths,' treating the places on the right of the decimal as mirrors that use the same names as the left side. Fix this by remembering that all place names to the right of the decimal end in '-ths,' signaling a fractional part.
untested_variants: Converting a decimal written in words into its fraction form — for example, writing 'forty-five hundredths' as the fraction $\frac{45}{100}$ and then simplifying it.
Comparing two decimals by reading them and deciding which is greater, such as determining whether $0.35$ or $0.350$ represents the same value and why.
Reading decimals that extend beyond the thousandths place, such as ten-thousandths ($0.0001$) or hundred-thousandths ($0.00001$).
Reading negative decimals, such as $-3.14$, and understanding how the negative sign combines with the decimal place-value name.
